text.title: Viral Loop Design Rules
text.summary: Actionable design rules for viral loops: K-factor category expectations, cycle time, invitee landing page priority, friction reduction, and embedded branding.

rule.statement: Anchor K-factor expectations to category benchmarks before designing — consumer apps: K=0.1–0.3; B2B SaaS: K=0.05–0.2. Designing for K > 0.5 leads to dark-pattern mechanics.
rule.rationale: K > 1 is rare and unstable; most successful products operate at K = 0.1–0.4 and use the viral lift to reduce CAC rather than achieve exponential growth.

rule.statement: Measure cycle time (p50 days from share to invitee activation) and treat it as a lever equal in importance to i and c — halving cycle time roughly squares compounding over a quarter.
rule.rationale: K = 0.4 with 3-day cycle time produces more users in 90 days than K = 0.7 with 60-day cycle time.

list.item: reduce cycle time: one-click signup for invitees, social login, pre-filled email from invite token
monitor cycle_time_p50 weekly; sudden increase signals friction introduced by a signup or onboarding change

rule.statement: Treat the invitee landing page as the highest-priority optimization surface — most loops fail at conversion, not at sharing.
rule.rationale: Improving the landing page conversion rate multiplies every future loop turn; share-side improvements only increase volume into the current conversion rate.

list.item: show inviter name and avatar prominently
display value preview before requiring signup
one-click signup (Google/Apple SSO or email magic link)
pre-fill email address from invite token
mobile-optimize: target <= 3 taps from invite link to activated account

rule.statement: Never require signup to view shared content; a preview wall converts better than a hard gate.
rule.rationale: Hard gates drop invitee conversion by 40–60%; previewing the value before requiring signup is the correct pattern for most content and social loops.

rule.statement: Embed branding in user output only where it adds value to the recipient (source credit, attribution) — not as a tax that degrades the artifact.
rule.rationale: Embedded branding used as a watermark without value to the recipient is perceived as spam; watermarks with source credit (Canva designs, Notion exports) are accepted because they provide context.

rule.statement: Plan to redesign embedded-branding loops every 12–18 months; users learn the pattern and conversion decays as familiarity removes novelty.
rule.rationale: Loops are not permanent — the pattern becomes ambient within 12–18 months and the invitee motivation ("what is this?") disappears.
